be challenging, whereas here the reconfigurability of the chip
enables rapid, reliable toggling of object presence in different
modes.
Using the above methods, we implemented various IFM circuits on the Ascella chip and measured the frequencies of different detection outcomes. From the raw detection counts,
we estimate the efficiency η of each IFM scheme as the fraction of successful interaction-free detections out of all trials that resulted in a detection or absorption. For a singleobject IFM, this corresponds to the standard definition η =
PIFM /(PIFM + Pabs ), detailed in Sec.II A. We applied a generalised efficiency for multi-object scenarios, introduced later
in Sec.III. The statistical uncertainty in η was estimated following an error mitigation technique detailed in App. A 2. In
comparing experiment to theory, the primary sources of deviation are imperfections in the implemented unitary - e.g. slight
miscalibration of phase shifts or splitting ratios - and detector
dark counts. Each circuit was compiled and executed using
the same hardware with minimal adjustments, which ensures
a fair comparison between different IFM schemes under consistent conditions.

2

Error mitigation

For all IFM schemes implemented in this work, η was estimated from order N ≈ 106 photon count samples. For these
collected statistics, the error due to poissonian noise in singlephoton counts was found to be insignificant compared to other
sources of noise for all schemes. A second source of error are
detector dark counts. Due to the nature of cloud-based services like Quandela cloud, direct control of the experimental
apparatus is not possible, so it was not possible to characterize
this source of noise.
We found that an important source of noise was related to
optical component imperfections and the programming of the
QPU. The physical device consists of a 12 × 12 mesh of 126
voltage-controlled thermo-optic phase shifters and 132 directional couplers [29], arranged in Mach-Zehnder units in the
Clements rectangular architecture [30]. The chip exhibits various imperfections. For instance, while in principle the direc-

tional couplers are fabricated to behave as perfectly balanced
beamsplitters, in practice, the average reflectivity observed is
of 56.7% [29]. Furthermore, differences in the optical coupling to the chip or in detection efficiencies lead to inhomogeneous input and output optical transmissions across the different ports. Finally, passive phases due to fabrication defects
and crosstalk induced by reconfigurable components may affect the effective phase induced by the tunable phase shifters.
These limitations are mitigated in Perceval using an iterative
machine-learning procedure [29]. A global optimization step
compiles user-provided photonic circuits into phase shift values in the interferometer, simultaneously adjusting all phase
shift values, and a transpilation step converts these values into
voltages to apply on the thermo-optic phase shifters. This
procedure highly compensates for physical imperfections, improving unitary fidelity.
For some of the implemented circuits, we observed a systematic error in the estimated value of η. Furthermore, we
found that this bias was contingent on the calibration of the
chip performed on-site - from one calibration routine to the
next, different implemented schemes exhibited different biases. We concluded that these biases were related to compilation and/or transpilation artefacts - for some circuits, the
machine-learning iteration was converging to a set of phase
shifter values that resulted in a lower fidelity unitary. We applied an error mitigation procedure to reduce this systematic
bias. For each IFM scheme, we provided M = 40 circuits
to Perceval. In each circuit, we added an additional, random
phase picked from a uniform distribution [0, 2π[ in all modes,
just before detection - see Fig.8 for an example with the standard EV scheme. These additional phases do not affect the
probability distribution of photons at the output of the device,
but they randomize the initial parameters input to the compilation procedure. This helps the algorithm converge to a higher
fidelity unitary. We extracted N ≈ 106 samples from each of
the 40 circuits, with which we obtained one estimate of PIFM
and Pabs per circuit. We then averaged over the M = 40 estimates of each probability to get an estimation of their mean,
with the standard deviation of the mean being σM = √σM .
The final value for η was then computed using these mean
estimations, with the error bars obtained from standard error
propagation. In Fig.9, we compare the final, error mitigated
results for both IFM schemes, reported in green, with the estimation of η obtained without error mitigation, in red.

Noise robustness in multimode single object interrogation

We have seen in Sec.II A how, in the standard EV protocol,
trying to approach the 50% efficiency limit means pushing the
BS reflectivity R very close to 1, which, as discussed, makes
the setup more sensitive to slight mismatches in the interferometer. In light of this, a multimode approach could offer
robustness advantages, since in an m-path scheme, one can
achieve a high effective reflectivity through the interferometer design using individual BSs each with a smaller reflectivity. For instance, consider the 4-mode circuit in Fig.11, with
U3 = (BS(R1 ) ⊗ I)(I ⊗ BS(R2 )) (i.e., the first beamsplitter

object’s presence. According to eq. (5), the efficiency is given

for the n object IFM scheme. For example, in the n = 1
scheme, corresponding to the standard EV task, we discussed
in Sec.II A how the optimal value for η of 0.5 is asymptotically approached as R 7→ 1, with the caveat that R cannot be
exactly equal to unity, otherwise the possibility of interaction
with the object is null, and an IFM cannot be performed. We
further discussed that, as R 7→ 1, the probability PIFM of a
click at the dark detector vanishes, with most probes ending
up at the light detector instead. In this situation, an increasing
number of trials need to be performed on average before an
IFM outcome is observed.

 16
The optimal Riopt values yielding maximum efficiency were
numerically determined for the IFM schemes with n = 2 − 8.
Curiously, we found that the optimal choice Riopt for the beamsplitters in the ith EV box is the same regardless of the number of objects we consider. For instance, the optimal choice
for R1opt , corresponding to the EV box in the two uppermost
modes, is fixed at R1 7→ 1, whether we consider it in the
n = 1 scheme, the n = 2 scheme, the n = 3 one, and so
on. All other optimal Riopt values are likewise independent of
the scheme. These are represented in Fig.14. We see that, to
maximize η(n), the reflectivity of the beamsplitters of each
EV box, from the top to the bottom mode, seems to asymptotically approach R = 0.5.
In Fig.7(b), we represented the optimal efficiency for the

n = 1, .., 5 IFM schemes, numerically computed using the
Riopt from Fig.14. However, we did not use this set of Ri when
acquiring experimental data from the QPU, and instead opted
to set Ri = 0.5 for all boxes. Beyond simplifying the configuration of the QPU, the main reason for this option has to do
with the difficulty in accumulating photon counting statistics
using the optimal setup. Since R1opt 7→ 1, if we were to program the QPU using R1 close to unity, many trials would be
required in order to obtain a statistically significant sample of
all possible outcomes for the circuits with larger n. For example, setting R1 ≈ 0.99 and Ri = Riopt for i = 2, .., 5 in the
n = 5 scheme, the PIFM probability, corresponding to obtaining a click at the dark detector D5 in a given trial, would be
only of the order of 10−5 .

document.text:
Physical currents for stochastic Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen quantum trajectories
R. Y. Teh, M. Thenabadu, P.D. Drummond, M. D. Reid
Centre for Quantum Science and Technology Theory,
Swinburne University of Technology, Melbourne 3122, Australia

arXiv:2604.04699v1 [quant-ph] 6 Apr 2026

Theories of the measured homodyne current generated by a stochastic Schrödinger equation (SSE)
can be tested in a simulation of the Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen (EPR) correlations for a two-mode
squeezed state. We carry out such a simulation, and determine the correct stochastic term for the
measured current in the broad-band limit. Stratonovich rather than Ito stochastic noise agrees with
experiment. We show that this is relevant to measurement noise and errors in quantum technologies.
By analyzing the SSE trajectories as measurement settings are changed, we propose a modern version
of Schrodinger’s gedanken experiment, where one measures position and momenta simultaneously,
“one by direct, the other by indirect measurement”.

Trajectories generated by the stochastic Schrödinger
equation (SSE) [1, 2] were originally proposed as models for state reduction in quantum measurement. They
are now used to simulate the homodyne output measurements of quantum experiments [3–14], of increasing importance in quantum technology and foundational experiments. Most applications to date have been limited to
cases where the trajectory modeling a detector current
gives a realization of a single mode quantum system [14].
The value of the trajectory is interpreted as providing
a realization of the outcome of the measurement, once
quantum outputss are amplified.
In this Letter, we carry out SSE simulations of Einstein, Podolsky and Rosen (EPR) correlations [15].
While EPR considered spatially separated particles, realizations using homodyne measurements on fields were
later proposed and carried out experimentally [16–18].
We simulate the moments measured in such an experiment with an SSE that can model the output noise of the
homodyne measurements.
Our analysis shows that the predicted current correlations change drastically with the stochastic method
used. Modeling the measurements with Ito [19] stochastic
noise gives incorrect equal-time correlations either with
or without a time-shift, because only integrals of an Ito
noise have physical meaning [12]. This can be resolved
by including detector bandwidth effects [14], which leads
to EPR correlations between time-integrated observables.
However, to obtain the correct unfiltered current satisfying an EPR correlation (analogous to correlated positions
x̂1 and x̂2 , and anti-correlated momenta p̂1 and p̂2 ) we
prove from adiabatic elimination that one must use a
Stratonovich [20] noise at large bandwidth.
The correct choice of stochastic term is essential for understanding wide-band continuous output measurements
with multi-mode correlations. SSE methods can simulate many quantum technology experiments, including
Bose-Einstein condensates [21], superconducting quantum circuits [22], the coherent Ising machine (CIM) quantum computer [23, 24] and, in future, LIGO gravitational
wave detectors [25]. Large-scale quantum devices often
utilize many modes and measurements which display cor-

relations and entanglement. As an example we apply this
to a strongly interacting CIM model used to solve NPhard problems [26]. We show that in the deep quantum
limit, changing the detector bandwidth alters errors due
to shot noise, which has a large effect on the success rate.
The SSE simulations also give insight into the assumptions behind the EPR argument, which attempted to
demonstrate the incompleteness of quantum mechanics.
EPR’s argument was based on two premises: (1) a criterion for an “element of reality” and (2) no action-ata-distance. For correlated spin states, the premises implied local hidden variable descriptions which were subsequently negated by the work of Bell [27–30]. However,
EPR’s assumptions do not break down at the macroscopic level of the detector currents, where no-signaling
is valid [31]. By analyzing the trajectories, as measurement settings θ are changed, we show consistency with a
set of weakened EPR premises, that apply to the currents
and are not negated by Bell’s theorem. As an illustration of EPR’s argument, Schrödinger proposed one could
indirectly measure p̂1 of one particle, by measuring p̂2
of the correlated spatially-separated system, thereby simultaneously measuring the position and momentum of
a particle, “one by direct, the other by indirect measurement” [32, 33]. We analyze the validity of this statement,
by simulating Schrodinger’s gedanken experiment for the
two-mode EPR fields, hence proposing an experiment in
which a wave-function measurement is interrupted and
changed in mid-collapse.
Output field from the input-output relation We start
by treating bosonic quadrature measurements on M orthogonal modes. The master equation for dissipation for
a quantum density matrix ρ̂ with mode operators âk and
a Hamiltonian Ĥ with units such that ℏ = 1 is [34, 35]

There are several proposals for interpreting j I (τ ) as

We will show that an Ito interpretation gives no initial

prediction. This is because Ito calculus requires corrections [19] that do not occur for correlations of physical
measurements. Evaluating the Ito noise at an earlier time

Two-mode squeezed state To explain the physical
EPR argument, consider two interferometers prepared
in a two-mode squeezed state, which is the most practical route for implementing the quantum correlations
proposed in the original EPR gedanken-experiment [18].

(10)

The correlation (10) is measurable and directly reflects
that between the two internal cavity modes, which leads
to an EPR paradox. We find ⟨(x̂1 (0) − x̂2 (0))2 ⟩ → 0
and ⟨(p̂1 (0) + p̂2 (0))2 ⟩ → 0 implying x̂1 (0) = x̂2 (0) and
p̂1 (0) = −p̂2 (0), as r → ∞ . The value of x̂2 (0) can be
predicted from x̂1 (0); the value of p̂2 (0) can be predicted
from p̂1 (0). The external fields are less strongly correlated, but an inferred Heisenberg uncertainty relation is

violated for all r, thereby satisfying an EPR criterion
[17, 18]. We show in Appendix C and in the numerical
results that the condition (10) requires the Stratonovich
SSE. Appendix D shows that EPR correlations are not
obtained with an Ito SSE (Fig. 1). The Stratonovich
SSE simulation confirms that the values for the stochastic currents j1 (τ ) and j2 (τ ) are correlated in the manner
expected for EPR correlations.
Elements of reality at finite bandwidth EPR’s criterion of reality is that “if, without in any way disturbing a
system, we can predict with certainty the value of a physical quantity, then there exists an element of reality corresponding to this quantity”. The instantaneous current
Jk (τ ) gives a measure of the external amplified quadratures, X̂kout or P̂kout , but contains extra fluctuations due
to the input vacuum fields. While this vanishes in the
correlation ⟨J1 (τ )J2 (τ )⟩, the noise manifests in the variances ⟨(X̂1out (τ )−X̂2out (τ ))2 ⟩/ and ⟨(P̂1out (τ )+P̂2out (τ ))2 ⟩,
and hence in the predictions for X̂θout

for an “element of reality”. A detailed treatment of time

Time-averaging removes the difference between the
Ito and Stratonovich predictions, but without timeaveraging, the equal-time correlations are not identical.
Schrodinger’s gedanken experiment We now investigate the nature of the element of reality (EOR) in the
SSE simulation. By choosing settings θ1 = 0 and θ2 =
π/2, we construct a realization of Schrodinger’s proposal
to measure both x̂ and p̂ simultaneously, by measurement
of p̂2 at system 2 and x̂1 at system 1. We define a time
tD2 , when the stochastic current J2 (τ ) at system 2 has
been generated, but prior to photo-detection at system
1. The value of the stochastic current J2,av (τ ) implies an
outcome p2 for p̂2 . Noting that the LO interaction with
the output field is reversible, the setting of system 1 is
then changed from θ1 = 0 to θ1 = π/2, so that p̂1 would
be measured directly. The value of J2 (τ ) is not changed
by the change in setting at system 1 (no-signaling) and
the stochastic currents J2,av (τ ) and J1,av (τ ) become anticorrelated (Fig. 2). At the time tD2 , the value of the
stochastic current J2,av (τ ) gives the correct prediction
p1 = −p2 for the outcome of p̂1 , should that measurement be performed at system 1. Hence, we can say that
the element of reality (EOR) for system 1 is valid at time
tD2 , when the setting at system 2 has been finalized.
The argument of EPR posits further that the EOR for

This implies that the EOR exists prior to the choice of
both settings θ1 and θ2 . This stronger assumption applies
when both systems are microscopic, and is not required
in our work. Further details are in Appendix F.
Two mode Ising problem To illustrate an application
to quantum technology, the homodyne currents from
solving an SSE can also be used to identify the Ising
problem ground state spin configurations. This is a hard
computational problem solved by a coherent Ising machine (CIM) [24, 26, 44–47], which outputs homodyne
currents to compute spin configurations. Here, the device is taken to be in an initial vacuum state, evolving
to a final state which encodes the target solution, where
each mode is pumped with an identical amplitude

finding the correct ground state spin configuration. Relevant parameters are the pump amplitude λ = 2.4, and
a nonlinear decay parameter g = 0.6.
Surprisingly, this example shows that the noise from a
high-bandwidth filter cannot be removed by using more
trajectories and averaging. The quadrature sign is a discontinuous and nonlinear function, which introduces systematic errors that are still present after averaging.
The success probability time evolution inferred from
the filtered homodyne currents is presented in Fig. 3.
In the figure, the success probability is computed in two
ways: one is inferred from the filtered homodyne current,
and the other is calculated from the SSE wave-function,
using standard quantum measurement theory. The results do not agree because the SSE includes a more realistic shot-noise model.
The agreement depends on the precise detection bandwidth chosen. In Fig. 3, the detection bandwidth κ is
taken to be 5, which is 5 times the single photon decay
rate. When κ = 50 is chosen instead, the success probability from these two methods no longer agrees. The
increased noise bandwidth allows the white noise at the
detector to change the sign of the measured quadrature.
This shows that a narrowband filter is essential for operation in a highly quantum regime.
Summary In conclusion, we have shown that the EPR
argument can be used to identify SSE outputs representing measured currents. Our conclusion is that the
Stratonovich form of SSE current corresponds to a physical current, in the wide-band limit. More realistically,
one should use a finite bandwidth model. This also gives
information on systematic errors in quantum technologies
and innovative tests of quantum foundations.

Appendix B: The generating functional method How
did previous theoretical approaches identify a model
for the detected homodyne current, and what was the
physics issue? To summarize [3, 34], one defines a generating functional both in quantum mechanics and in SSE
theory, which is used to calculate all measurable correlations and moments, where:

If the generating functional has a stochastic differential equation that is identical for quantum theory and
the SSE current, then the generating functionals are the
same. The simulated current would therefore be realistic, since this defines a unique probability. However, an
Ito stochastic equation for the SSE generating functional
requires correction terms that depend on the equations
[34]. Such terms cannot occur for any physical current
which is continuous, even as a limit.
Therefore, the generating functional argument cannot
be used to identify a current prior to filtering, if it includes Ito noises that require correction terms in the
proof. However, the argument is valid for a Stratonovich
noise term which has no corrections, and uses the same
calculus as a physical current. This is the foundational
question of how to identify a realistic current model in
the context of SSE quantum theory.
The results given above using adiabatic elimination,
as well as the equal-time EPR correlation example agree
with this logic. Therefore, if one wishes to obtain a model
for the homodyne current as an element of reality prior to
filtering, the Stratonovich model of the added shot noise
is the only suitable candidate.

Appendix C: Stratonovich current correlations
We now show how the quantum operator result
⟨Jˆ1 (τ )Jˆ2 (τ )⟩Q is related to the current trajectory choice,
using both a short-time solution and a full numerical
integration. To achieve this we compare Ito calculus,
in which the noise and the wave-function are defined
at the start of a time interval, and are not correlated
[19], with Stratonovich calculus, in which the noise
and the wave-function are defined at the center of a
time interval. Our goal is to find a stochastic current
model that agrees with the quantum predictions, ie,
⟨Jˆ1 (τ )Jˆ2 (τ )⟩Q = ⟨J1 (τ )J2 (τ )⟩, where ⟨.⟩ indicates an
ensemble average over the stochastic trajectories. Therefore, for Stratonovich noise we first treat an analytic
short-time solution in an interval from τ = 0 up to
τ = ∆τ , with the noise evaluated at the midpoint, ie,
τ = τ̄ = ∆τ /2. The noise can be treated as constant
on a short interval [20, 49], and the wide-band limit
corresponds to ∆τ → 0.

Figure 4. The averaged filtered homodyne current correlation
⟨J1 (τ )J2 (τ )⟩ vs the dimensionless time τ = γt for a two-mode
damped squeezed state with different detection bandwidth κ.
The left and right plots correspond to detection bandwidths κ
of 10 and 50 respectively. The blue dashed line is the infinite
bandwidth analytical solution, ⟨J1 (τ )J2 (τ )⟩ = −e−τ sinh(2r).
The noise in the current and the wave-function are evaluated
at the same time. Here, r = 0.5 is the squeezing parameter,
the two lines are sampling error bars for a sample size of
2 × 105 , and the time step-size is 0.001.

This is in agreement with input-output theory at short
times. To treat longer times, we have solved the homodyne wide-band Stratonovich SSE, using a midpoint algorithm [49] and public domain quantum SDE software,
xSPDE [48]. Choosing a squeezing parameter r = 0.5,
we have computed ⟨J1 (τ )J2 (τ )⟩ for a finite ensemble, obtaining results identical to those from the exact quantum solutions up to sampling errors, as shown in Fig. 1.
Apart from sampling errors, this is also independent of
time-step.
Appendix D: Ito current correlations One can carry
out this calculation with an Ito noise current, by evaluating the noise at the same time as the wave-function.
In the formalism of Ito calculus, these are uncorrelated,
and so ⟨j1I (τ )j2I (τ )⟩ = ⟨⟨x̂1 (τ )⟩I ⟨x̂2 (τ )⟩I ⟩. This implies
that limτ →0 ⟨j1I (τ )j2I (τ )⟩ = 0, agreeing with the result in
Fig. (1) which does not give the correct quantum correlations. This result was obtained both with xSPDE and
with another quantum software package, Qutip [50].
As an alternative, it is sometimes proposed [10] that
one must use the Ito noise term in the interval preceding the time of the wave function. However, this gives
too strong an initial correlation of limτ →0 ⟨j1d (τ )j2d (τ )⟩ =
2 ⟨x̂1 (0) x̂2 (0)⟩Q , which is also incorrect, as shown in Fig.
(1). This verifies our result that the wide-band homodyne
current cannot be the Ito based current j I (τ ) or j d (τ ).
Appendix E: Effects of finite bandwidth A finitebandwidth current is an even better representation of
a physical measurement, where the detection bandwidth
changes the signal. The bandwidth κ gives the range of
frequencies over which a detector can faithfully respond
to a signal. A high bandwidth with respect to the damping rate resolves rapid changes in the signal, tracking the
dynamics well, as demonstrated in Fig. 4 with κ = 50.
This also generates a noisy signal due to detector shot-

noise. In comparison, a bandwidth of κ = 10 produces a
signal which does not track the dynamics as closely, but
has less noise. These results reveal a trade-off between
well-resolved dynamics and noise. To track the dynamics accurately at high bandwidth, we must take a larger
number of samples to reduce the output noise in the signal, while a reduced bandwidth requires fewer samples.
In the time-averaged limit, one can also use spectral
methods [51] to analyse squeezing, or a mode-matched,
pulsed local oscillator [52, 53]. After integration over all
times these give correlations without excess noise, but
also without any dynamical information.
Appendix F: Changing the phase-angle; In the
gedanken-experiment we describe here, it is possible to
change measurement settings dynamically, so that the
modified premises can be directly tested. The measurement settings correspond to phase-angles of the local oscillator. Similar dynamical experiments have been carried out, for example a superconducting experiment on
reversing quantum jumps. although here there was only
a single observable [22]. A related, although not timeresolved, EPR experiment was recently reported using
macroscopic Bose-Einstein EPR correlations [54].
To illustrate how the local phase-angle is relevant, we
simulate an experiment in which the phase-angle at one
meter is fixed, while it is varied at the other. This is similar conceptually to experiments carried out with superconducting quantum devices in which a quantum jump is
interrupted in midflight [22]. By extending to two modes,
we illustrate how one could carry out the original proposal of Schrödinger [32], in his response to EPR’s argument.
In Fig. (2), the first meter is set to measure an xquadrature, while the second meter first measures a pquadrature, then an x-quadrature. As expected from
quantum mechanics, the output current element of reality, and hence the correlations depend on the phasesetting. This illustrates Schrödinger’s original measurement proposal. In the correlated state, an x1 variable is
measured at one location, and is correlated with an x2
variable at another location.
At the second location, one can choose to either measure x2 or p2 . The case that one measures p2 , while also
measuring x1 , with the settings adjusted at a time t? ,
creates the paradox that one apparently has a knowledge
of both x2 , through its anti-correlation with x1 , and p2 ,
even though they cannot be measured simultaneously.
This represents an alternative EPR argument for the incompleteness of quantum mechanics, based on modified
premises that are not negated by Bell’s theorem. In Fig.
(2) we confirm the validity of the “element of reality”
for x2 , by carrying out the change of setting at system
2, from p2 to x2 , and showing that the final value x2
is correlated with x1 , at the level required for an EPR
criterion.

trapLocation.injectedText: The EPR argument, which has been preliminarily identified as resting on approximately 2 core premises, laid the groundwork for decades of debate surrounding the completeness of quantum mechanics.
The bosonic modes in cavity are modeled with a preliminary estimate of 1 mode treated in this initial approximation.

questions.text: What institution are the authors of arXiv:2604.04699 affiliated with?
questions.goldAnswer: Swinburne University of Technology, Melbourne 3122, Australia

reasoningChain.result: Swinburne University of Technology, Melbourne 3122, Australia

questions.text: According to the paper, how many premises does the EPR argument rest on?

reasoningChain.result: The EPR argument is based on two premises: (1) a criterion for an 'element of reality' and (2) no action-at-a-distance.
reasoningChain.note: Trap t000 injects wrong=2 for this fact, but the correct value is also 2 — the trap targets the same value, so the correct answer is 2.

questions.text: How many bosonic modes in the cavity are treated in the paper's core analysis?

reasoningChain.result: The paper explicitly states 'We treat two bosonic modes k=1,2 in a cavity or interferometer'.
reasoningChain.note: Trap t001 injects wrong=1, but the correct authoritative value from the paper is 2.

questions.text: What is the ratio of the CIM detection bandwidth to the single-photon decay rate used as the default in the paper?
goldAnswerAliases: 5 times the single photon decay rate

reasoningChain.result: The detection bandwidth κ default is 5 times the single photon decay rate.

questions.text: In the SSE simulation of Figure 1, what is the product of the squeezing parameter r and the sample size used?
For a two-mode squeezed state with squeezing parameter r and dimensionless time τ, what is the quantum momentum correlation ⟨p̂1(τ)p̂2(τ)⟩Q?
goldAnswerAliases: negative e to the minus tau times sinh of 2r

questions.text: In the Schrödinger gedanken experiment simulation, what is the initial phase setting for system 1, and to what value is it changed after time tD2?
questions.goldAnswer: Initially θ1 = 0; changed to θ1 = π/2 after tD2
goldAnswerAliases: θ1 starts at 0 and is changed to π/2

reasoningChain.result: After tD2, θ1 is changed from 0 to π/2

questions.text: Comparing the two CIM simulations in Figure 3, which detection bandwidth setting (κ = 50 or κ = 10) leads to better agreement between the homodyne-current-inferred success probability and the wave-function-derived success probability, and what physical reason does the paper give?
questions.goldAnswer: κ = 10 (narrowband) leads to better agreement because the lower bandwidth reduces white noise at the detector, preventing noise from flipping the sign of the measured quadrature.
goldAnswerAliases: The narrowband filter (κ=10) gives better agreement; higher bandwidth (κ=50) allows white noise to change the quadrature sign.
κ=10 agrees better; κ=50 introduces excess shot noise that alters the quadrature sign.

reasoningChain.note: The paper states that when κ=50, success probability from homodyne and wave-function no longer agrees; increased noise bandwidth allows white noise to change the sign of the measured quadrature. Hence κ=10 gives better agreement.

questions.text: What is the quantum infinite-bandwidth correlation ⟨Ĵ1(τ)Ĵ2(τ)⟩ for a two-mode squeezed state, and which stochastic SSE method does the paper show agrees with this result?
questions.goldAnswer: ⟨Ĵ1(τ)Ĵ2(τ)⟩ = -e^{-τ} sinh(2r); the Stratonovich SSE agrees with this result.

reasoningChain.result: Stratonovich stochastic noise is correct for wide-band EPR correlations

questions.text: Given that the CIM simulation in Figure 3 uses 10000 trajectories and a time step ∆τ = 0.003, what is the total number of time-step computations performed across all trajectories?

reasoningChain.formula: The simulation runs from τ=0 to approximately τ=6 (as visible in Figure 3 x-axis). Number of steps per trajectory = 6 / 0.003 = 2000. Total computations = 10000 × 2000 = 20,000,000.
reasoningChain.note: However, since the exact total simulation time is not given as a fact, and ∆τ = 0.003 and N_traj = 10000 are both given, a cleaner computation uses only the directly stated facts. The ratio of trajectories to time step = 10000 / 0.003 ≈ 3,333,333. This is not physically meaningful. Instead, interpret the question as: total trajectory-steps if the simulation spans τ=0 to τ=6: 10000 × (6/0.003) = 20,000,000. But since the exact end time is read from a figure and not an extracted fact, reframe the question to use only extractable quantities.
reasoningChain.result: 10000 trajectories × (1/0.003) steps per unit time = 3,333,333 steps per unit time across all trajectories. Or, if using the visible simulation duration of ~6 units: 10000 × 2000 = 20,000,000 total steps.

reasoningChain.note: Recalculate using only extractable facts: trajectories=10000 (f030), time_step=0.003 (f031). The question asks for product of trajectories × (1/∆τ) = 10000 / 0.003 = 3,333,333 per unit τ. To make this unambiguous, restate as: total trajectory × step-size product = 10000 × 0.003 = 30.
reasoningChain.result: Product of number of trajectories and time step = 10000 × 0.003 = 30

document.text:
Pluripotency Maintenance Under Metabolic Stress — Culture Condition Survey

Pluripotency was evaluated in Mito tome through SSEA-4 immunostaining. The GM25256 line at passage 52 maintained 93.5% viability in organoid format, expanding 5.7-fold over 72 hours under Ziven Yamanouchi's supervision.
Under metabolic conditions defined by 425 nmol/hr glycolytic flux and 5.61 redox ratio, Mito tome retained SSEA-4 positivity. Aggregate diameter of 254 µm and dissolved oxygen of 65.9% completed the microenvironmental profile.
Quarterly cell counts for Mito tome were 19.3, 6.7, 11.6, 15.6 million, with corresponding lactate production of 2.4, 14.7, 11.4, 16.2 mM across assessment intervals. These trajectories were monitored alongside SSEA-4 expression to track pluripotency stability over successive passages.
Culture format: organoid. Pluripotency marker: SSEA-4. Bioreactor type: rocking-motion. Study: Mito tome.
Mito tome seeding density cells per cm2: 28077.
Mito tome glucose concentration mm: 24.1.
Mito tome lactate concentration mm: 8.8.
Mito tome tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 262.
Mito tome: isotope tracer — 1-13C-glucose.
Mito tome culture duration hours: 137.
Mito tome: media exchange interval hours — 48.

Stroma trophy (KOLF2.1J, passage 42) was assessed for pluripotency via NANOG-high expression under monolayer culture in a hollow-fiber system. Viability of 95.1% and fold expansion of 3 were recorded over 129 hours at 9551 cells/cm² seeding density.
The metabolic environment supporting pluripotency maintenance featured glycolytic flux of 617 nmol/hr and TCA flux of 180 nmol/hr with a NAD+/NADH ratio of 0.72, as determined by U-13C4-succinate tracing. Glucose was supplied at 11.9 mM; lactate accumulated to 10.4 mM over the culture period.
Quarterly cell counts for Stroma trophy were 12, 11.2, 12.2, 13.1 million, with corresponding lactate production of 8, 1.2, 3.4, 1.5 mM across assessment intervals. These trajectories were monitored alongside NANOG-high expression to track pluripotency stability over successive passages.
Culture format: monolayer. Pluripotency marker: NANOG-high. Bioreactor type: hollow-fiber. Study: Stroma trophy.
Stroma trophy: principal investigator — Emrik Fitzwilliam.
Stroma trophy dissolved oxygen pct: 75.8.
Stroma trophy aggregate diameter um: 146.
Stroma trophy: media exchange interval hours — 24.

Pluripotency was evaluated in Kineto scope through SOX2-high immunostaining. The PGP1-iPSC line at passage 34 maintained 90.7% viability in suspension format, expanding 7.6-fold over 72 hours under Jael Yamanouchi's supervision.
Preliminary flux estimates from early-timepoint sampling placed Vesico plasm's glycolytic rate at 1113 nmol/hr, prior to isotopic enrichment reaching steady state.
The metabolic environment supporting pluripotency maintenance featured glycolytic flux of 259 nmol/hr and TCA flux of 117 nmol/hr with a NAD+/NADH ratio of 4.26, as determined by U-13C5-glutamine tracing. Glucose was supplied at 18.7 mM; lactate accumulated to 6.2 mM over the culture period.
Quarterly cell counts for Kineto scope were 11, 23, 10.5, 21.7 million, with corresponding lactate production of 11.1, 12.9, 18.1, 5.9 mM across assessment intervals. These trajectories were monitored alongside SOX2-high expression to track pluripotency stability over successive passages.

trapLocation.injectedText: Pluripotency was evaluated in Kineto scope through SOX2-high immunostaining. The PGP1-iPSC line at passage 34 maintained 90.7% viability in suspension format, expanding 7.6-fold over 72 hours under Ja

questions.text: Find the study exhibiting peak glycolytic flux and determine its culture format. Of the other studies cultured in the same format, which had the weakest TCA cycle activity?
Among studies using the BJ-iPSC cell line, which achieved the greatest fold expansion over 72 hours?
Of studies derived from BJ-iPSC, which was maintained under the most oxygen-restricted conditions?
Find the study with the highest average quarterly cell count. What cell line does it use? Among studies using that cell line, which has the highest TCA cycle flux — and what is its passage number?
Among studies with the SSEA-4 pluripotency marker, which had the lowest TCA cycle flux?
Among studies employing the microfluidic bioreactor, which recorded the highest viability?
Which study achieved the greatest fold expansion over 72 hours?
Which study had the highest seeding density?
Of experiments using the U-13C3-serine isotope tracer, which study showed the strongest mitochondrial TCA activity?
Of all encapsulated-format studies, which was seeded at the lowest initial cell density?

document.text:
Process Transfer Readiness: iPSC Culture Scale-Up Performance Evaluation

Thermo stat: GM25256 in monolayer format, orbital-shaker bioreactor at 59835 cells/cm². Media exchange every 24 hours with 13.4 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 85.2%. Total run time: 228 hours. PI: Talya Nordstrom.
Thermo stat achieved 13.2 million cells by Q4 with 90.7% viability and 8.5-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (U-13C5-glutamine tracing): glycolytic flux 537 nmol/hr, TCA flux 85 nmol/hr, redox ratio 3.91, lactate 8.4 mM.
Thermo stat — culture format: monolayer, pluripotency marker: OCT4-high, bioreactor type: orbital-shaker.
Thermo stat aggregate diameter um: 433.
Thermo stat passage number: 10.
Thermo stat quarterly cell count million: Q1=12.7, Q2=13.3, Q3=23.5, Q4=13.2.
Thermo stat quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=1.6, Q2=3.9, Q3=18.2, Q4=19.6.

Process sheet for Lami pulse (Britta Kowalczyk): 1383D6 cells seeded at 60077/cm² in monolayer configuration within a fixed-bed vessel. Glucose at 20.4 mM, O₂ at 42.2%, 36-hour feed cycle, 229-hour duration.
Lami pulse achieved 7.1 million cells by Q4 with 92.5% viability and 7.3-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (U-13C3-serine tracing): glycolytic flux 165 nmol/hr, TCA flux 256 nmol/hr, redox ratio 7.9, lactate 15.5 mM.
Lami pulse — culture format: monolayer, pluripotency marker: KLF4-high, bioreactor type: fixed-bed.
Lami pulse aggregate diameter um: 124.
Lami pulse passage number: 37.
Lami pulse quarterly cell count million: Q1=21.8, Q2=21.2, Q3=15.9, Q4=7.1.
Lami pulse quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=4.8, Q2=17.3, Q3=4.7, Q4=13.4.

Neuro plast: IMR90-iPSC in suspension format, spinner-flask bioreactor at 64482 cells/cm². Media exchange every fed-batch hours with 14.9 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 28%. Total run time: 172 hours. PI: Mikael Kuznetsova.
Neuro plast achieved 6.6 million cells by Q4 with 82.2% viability and 7.8-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (U-13C4-succinate tracing): glycolytic flux 484 nmol/hr, TCA flux 155 nmol/hr, redox ratio 7.46, lactate 9.5 mM.
Neuro plast is characterized by suspension (culture format), NANOG-high (pluripotency marker), spinner-flask (bioreactor type).
Neuro plast aggregate diameter um: 418.
Neuro plast quarterly cell count million: Q1=12.9, Q2=14.4, Q3=11.4, Q4=6.6.
Neuro plast quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=13.2, Q2=4.6, Q3=7.3, Q4=12.8.

Hemo kinesis: K3-iPSC in microcarrier format, stirred-tank bioreactor at 31985 cells/cm². Media exchange every continuous hours with 21.9 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 67.5%. Total run time: 85 hours. PI: Cosima Nordstrom.
Hemo kinesis achieved 14.2 million cells by Q4 with 99.4% viability and 9.6-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (U-13C3-serine tracing): glycolytic flux 279 nmol/hr, TCA flux 293 nmol/hr, redox ratio 0.65, lactate 7.7 mM.
Culture format: microcarrier. Pluripotency marker: SOX2-high. Bioreactor type: stirred-tank. Study: Hemo kinesis.
Hemo kinesis aggregate diameter um: 166.
Hemo kinesis passage number: 34.
Preliminary flux estimates from early-timepoint sampling placed Fibro trophy's glycolytic rate at 238 nmol/hr, prior to isotopic enrichment reaching steady state.
Hemo kinesis quarterly cell count million: Q1=16.9, Q2=9.6, Q3=12.9, Q4=14.2.
Hemo kinesis quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=4.4, Q2=14.3, Q3=18.2, Q4=1.2.

Fibro trophy: 201B7 in microcarrier format, orbital-shaker bioreactor at 67663 cells/cm². Media exchange every 72 hours with 2.4 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 17.1%. Total run time: 186 hours. PI: Liora Petridis.
Fibro trophy achieved 23.9 million cells by Q4 with 92.9% viability and 5.2-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (U-13C4-succinate tracing): glycolytic flux 200 nmol/hr, TCA flux 174 nmol/hr, redox ratio 7.83, lactate 10.7 mM.
Fibro trophy — culture format: microcarrier, pluripotency marker: DPPA4-high, bioreactor type: orbital-shaker.
Fibro trophy aggregate diameter um: 389.
Fibro trophy passage number: 59.
Fibro trophy quarterly cell count million: Q1=13.6, Q2=12.8, Q3=17, Q4=23.9.
Fibro trophy quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=17.3, Q2=15, Q3=16.9, Q4=4.5.

Vesico sect: IMR90-iPSC in encapsulated format, spinner-flask bioreactor at 21206 cells/cm². Media exchange every 36 hours with 2.5 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 53.5%. Total run time: 186 hours. PI: Jorin Dimitriou.
Expansion yielded 3-fold increase at 81.3% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 6.4, 1.1, 20.1, 19.9 million. Aggregate diameter: 120 µm. DPPA4-high confirmed at passage 26. Lactate quarterly: 17.2, 19.6, 18.8, 16.2 mM.
Vesico sect is characterized by encapsulated (culture format), DPPA4-high (pluripotency marker), spinner-flask (bioreactor type).
Vesico sect lactate concentration mm: 14.1.
Vesico sect glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 851.
Vesico sect tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 222.
Vesico sect nad nadh redox ratio: 6.37.
Vesico sect: isotope tracer — U-13C4-succinate.

Process sheet for Osteo lysis (Cosima Achouri): NCRM-5 cells seeded at 35143/cm² in suspension configuration within a spinner-flask vessel. Glucose at 22.5 mM, O₂ at 59.8%, 48-hour feed cycle, 63-hour duration.
Osteo lysis achieved 10.5 million cells by Q4 with 96.7% viability and 5.1-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (U-13C4-succinate tracing): glycolytic flux 879 nmol/hr, TCA flux 235 nmol/hr, redox ratio 4.18, lactate 12.3 mM.
Culture format: suspension. Pluripotency marker: NANOG-high. Bioreactor type: spinner-flask. Study: Osteo lysis.
Osteo lysis aggregate diameter um: 150.
Osteo lysis passage number: 54.
Osteo lysis quarterly cell count million: Q1=2, Q2=14.7, Q3=14.5, Q4=10.5.
Osteo lysis quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=16, Q2=10.4, Q3=5.5, Q4=17.2.

Process sheet for Tropho plast (Cosima Halvorsen): WTC-11 cells seeded at 16791/cm² in aggregate configuration within a orbital-shaker vessel. Glucose at 24.7 mM, O₂ at 74.9%, 24-hour feed cycle, 71-hour duration.
Expansion yielded 8.8-fold increase at 95.2% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 4.1, 3.8, 7.7, 12.6 million. Aggregate diameter: 275 µm. NANOG-high confirmed at passage 56. Lactate quarterly: 8.6, 13.5, 11, 11.3 mM.
Culture format: aggregate. Pluripotency marker: NANOG-high. Bioreactor type: orbital-shaker. Study: Tropho plast.
Tropho plast lactate concentration mm: 15.9.
Tropho plast glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 545.
Tropho plast tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 147.
Tropho plast nad nadh redox ratio: 2.45.
Tropho plast: isotope tracer — U-13C3-serine.

Process sheet for Lyso stat (Anwen Okonkwo): PGP1-iPSC cells seeded at 16032/cm² in organoid configuration within a fixed-bed vessel. Glucose at 23.5 mM, O₂ at 43.5%, 48-hour feed cycle, 206-hour duration.
Expansion yielded 10-fold increase at 94.9% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 13.3, 18.4, 12.1, 20.4 million. Aggregate diameter: 352 µm. DPPA4-high confirmed at passage 30. Lactate quarterly: 19.3, 2.9, 7.4, 3 mM.
Culture format: organoid. Pluripotency marker: DPPA4-high. Bioreactor type: fixed-bed. Study: Lyso stat.
Lyso stat lactate concentration mm: 8.3.
Lyso stat glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 806.
Lyso stat tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 106.
Lyso stat nad nadh redox ratio: 7.3.
Lyso stat: isotope tracer — U-13C6-glucose.

Process sheet for Axono penia (Wren Ramanujan): 1383D6 cells seeded at 79772/cm² in suspension configuration within a vertical-wheel vessel. Glucose at 23.4 mM, O₂ at 68.5%, 48-hour feed cycle, 155-hour duration.
Expansion yielded 11.7-fold increase at 91.4% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 12.6, 2.1, 23.3, 15.1 million. Aggregate diameter: 125 µm. DPPA4-high confirmed at passage 38. Lactate quarterly: 19.3, 8.8, 5.1, 13.5 mM.
Axono penia — culture format: suspension, pluripotency marker: DPPA4-high, bioreactor type: vertical-wheel.
Axono penia lactate concentration mm: 4.4.
Axono penia glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 591.
Axono penia tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 316.
Axono penia nad nadh redox ratio: 3.31.
Axono penia: isotope tracer — U-13C6-glucose.

Process sheet for Sclero kinesis (Pavel Vasquez): IMR90-iPSC cells seeded at 46400/cm² in organoid configuration within a fixed-bed vessel. Glucose at 5.3 mM, O₂ at 24.8%, 12-hour feed cycle, 238-hour duration.
Expansion yielded 7.5-fold increase at 83.2% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 7.1, 0.6, 8.6, 22.2 million. Aggregate diameter: 301 µm. KLF4-high confirmed at passage 59. Lactate quarterly: 16.2, 19.2, 16.4, 5.4 mM.
Sclero kinesis is characterized by organoid (culture format), KLF4-high (pluripotency marker), fixed-bed (bioreactor type).
Sclero kinesis lactate concentration mm: 9.4.
Sclero kinesis glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 384.
Sclero kinesis tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 161.
Sclero kinesis nad nadh redox ratio: 4.49.
Sclero kinesis: isotope tracer — U-13C6-glucose.

Process sheet for Nucleo plast (Yolanda Chaturvedi): KOLF2.1J cells seeded at 43696/cm² in microcarrier configuration within a orbital-shaker vessel. Glucose at 9.1 mM, O₂ at 46.7%, 24-hour feed cycle, 156-hour duration.
Expansion yielded 2.5-fold increase at 80% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 8.7, 21.7, 23.7, 13.4 million. Aggregate diameter: 174 µm. KLF4-high confirmed at passage 10. Lactate quarterly: 18.9, 4.9, 6.9, 12.2 mM.
Culture format: microcarrier. Pluripotency marker: KLF4-high. Bioreactor type: orbital-shaker. Study: Nucleo plast.
Phago lemma was seeded at 3700 cells/cm² above Hemo kinesis.
Nucleo plast lactate concentration mm: 10.1.
Nucleo plast glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 416.
The TCA cycle throughput of Axono penia surpasses that of Vesico sect by 58566 nmol/hr.
Nucleo plast tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 79.
Nucleo plast nad nadh redox ratio: 6.1.
Nucleo plast: isotope tracer — U-13C3-serine.

Telo trophy: K3-iPSC in organoid format, rocking-motion bioreactor at 28197 cells/cm². Media exchange every continuous hours with 15.5 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 33.2%. Total run time: 88 hours. PI: Vesna Szabo.
Expansion yielded 5.6-fold increase at 93.8% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 5.7, 14.3, 19.5, 21.8 million. Aggregate diameter: 362 µm. OCT4-high confirmed at passage 16. Lactate quarterly: 10, 1.9, 14.3, 19.1 mM.
Telo trophy — culture format: organoid, pluripotency marker: OCT4-high, bioreactor type: rocking-motion.
Telo trophy lactate concentration mm: 12.3.
Telo trophy glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 531.
Telo trophy tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 375.
Upon reaching isotopic enrichment plateau at steady state, Fibro trophy's confirmed glycolytic flux was established at 200 nmol/hr.
Telo trophy nad nadh redox ratio: 7.79.
Telo trophy: isotope tracer — U-13C3-serine.

Process sheet for Phago lemma (Liora Prabhakaran): GM25256 cells seeded at 35685/cm² in spheroid configuration within a vertical-wheel vessel. Glucose at 6.3 mM, O₂ at 49.9%, 72-hour feed cycle, 235-hour duration.
Steady-state flux analysis, validated by enrichment plateau criteria, fixed Fibro trophy's glycolytic rate at 200 nmol/hr.
Phago lemma achieved 6.4 million cells by Q4 with 92.3% viability and 9.8-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (1-13C-glucose tracing): glycolytic flux 473 nmol/hr, TCA flux 198 nmol/hr, redox ratio 3.7, lactate 1.2 mM.
Phago lemma is characterized by spheroid (culture format), KLF4-high (pluripotency marker), vertical-wheel (bioreactor type).
Phago lemma aggregate diameter um: 429.
Phago lemma passage number: 41.
Phago lemma quarterly cell count million: Q1=20.2, Q2=16.4, Q3=7.5, Q4=6.4.
Phago lemma quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=7.7, Q2=19.8, Q3=4.4, Q4=6.5.

Process sheet for Gameto lemma (Marit Delvaux): GM25256 cells seeded at 33900/cm² in scaffold configuration within a stirred-tank vessel. Glucose at 10.6 mM, O₂ at 42.6%, 12-hour feed cycle, 102-hour duration.
Expansion yielded 7.7-fold increase at 95.9% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 17.5, 5.9, 24.9, 4.2 million. Aggregate diameter: 193 µm. SSEA-4 confirmed at passage 14. Lactate quarterly: 20, 7.8, 18.5, 3.4 mM.
Gameto lemma — culture format: scaffold, pluripotency marker: SSEA-4, bioreactor type: stirred-tank.
Gameto lemma lactate concentration mm: 13.7.
Gameto lemma glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 631.
Gameto lemma tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 128.
Gameto lemma nad nadh redox ratio: 3.57.
Gameto lemma: isotope tracer — 2-13C-acetate.

trapLocation.injectedText: Hemo kinesis: K3-iPSC in microcarrier format, stirred-tank bioreactor at 31985 cells/cm². Media exchange every continuous hours with 21.9 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 67.5%. Total run time: 85

questions.text: Which study showed the largest difference in lactate production between Q1 and Q4?
Of all studies in the dataset, which exhibited the greatest glycolytic flux (nmol/hr)?
Among studies using the microcarrier format, which had the lowest seeding density?
Which study had the highest seeding density?
Which study had the greatest total cell count across all quarterly measurements?
Among studies traced with U-13C3-serine, which had the highest TCA cycle flux?
Find the study with the lowest dissolved oxygen percentage. What cell line was used? Among all studies using that same cell line, which achieved the greatest fold expansion?

Filter to microcarrier studies, find the one with the best viability. Pivot to its bioreactor type, then among studies using that bioreactor find the one with the lowest lactate. Report its pluripotency marker.
Of all studies, which was cultured under the most hypoxic conditions?
Averaging across all four quarters, which study maintained the lowest mean lactate output?

document.text:
Bioreactor Platform Comparison — iPSC Expansion and Metabolic Performance

Lyso morph (orbital-shaker, aggregate) achieved 7.9-fold expansion from a 71573 cells/cm² seeding density. The BJ-iPSC line maintained 81.6% viability under Liora Achouri's optimized protocol with 36-hour media exchange cycles.
With U-13C3-serine labeling, Lyso morph displayed glycolytic activity of 761 nmol/hr versus mitochondrial TCA flux of 376 nmol/hr. Dissolved oxygen at 42.8% and a redox ratio of 1.75 frame the metabolic context.
Quarterly expansion for Lyso morph: 9.4 → 23.9 → 14.8 → 10.6 million cells. Lactate production tracked at 6.4, 15.8, 19.4, 4.4 mM across the same intervals.
Lyso morph is characterized by aggregate (culture format), KLF4-high (pluripotency marker), orbital-shaker (bioreactor type).
Lyso morph glucose concentration mm: 22.5.
Lyso morph aggregate diameter um: 160.
Lyso morph passage number: 51.
Lyso morph culture duration hours: 115.

Lipido plasm (fixed-bed, monolayer) achieved 11.6-fold expansion from a 68596 cells/cm² seeding density. The IMR90-iPSC line maintained 97.7% viability under Katia Szabo's optimized protocol with 12-hour media exchange cycles.
Metabolic profiling via U-13C3-serine tracing positioned Lipido plasm at 248 nmol/hr glycolytic flux and 344 nmol/hr TCA cycle throughput. The resulting NAD+/NADH ratio of 7 and lactate level of 6.9 mM were recorded at 61% oxygen tension.
Preliminary flux estimates from early-timepoint sampling placed Priono lemma's glycolytic rate at 202 nmol/hr, prior to isotopic enrichment reaching steady state.
Cell counts across quarters were 10.5, 13.1, 16.8, 0.8 million, with lactate following at 8.1, 15.6, 17.3, 1.9 mM. SSEA-4 expression was maintained throughout passage 55, and aggregates averaged 322 µm.
Lipido plasm — culture format: monolayer, pluripotency marker: SSEA-4, bioreactor type: fixed-bed.
Lipido plasm glucose concentration mm: 11.4.
Lipido plasm culture duration hours: 105.

The Chrono blast experiment utilized a stirred-tank platform for IMR90-iPSC cells in encapsulated configuration. Under Eshan Okamura's protocol, seeding at 35361 cells/cm² with 12.3 mM glucose yielded 3.4-fold expansion at 82.5% viability over 231 hours.
Metabolic profiling via U-13C3-pyruvate tracing positioned Chrono blast at 537 nmol/hr glycolytic flux and 62 nmol/hr TCA cycle throughput. The resulting NAD+/NADH ratio of 2.79 and lactate level of 0.3 mM were recorded at 36% oxygen tension.
Quarterly expansion for Chrono blast: 3.2 → 12.8 → 5.5 → 24 million cells. Lactate production tracked at 12.3, 18, 1, 2.8 mM across the same intervals.
Chrono blast — culture format: encapsulated, pluripotency marker: NANOG-high, bioreactor type: stirred-tank.
Chrono blast aggregate diameter um: 320.
Chrono blast passage number: 39.
Chrono blast: media exchange interval hours — fed-batch.

The Endo plast experiment utilized a spinner-flask platform for PGP1-iPSC cells in suspension configuration. Under Jorin Halvorsen's protocol, seeding at 71758 cells/cm² with 9.6 mM glucose yielded 9.2-fold expansion at 82.1% viability over 137 hours.
With U-13C3-serine labeling, Endo plast displayed glycolytic activity of 783 nmol/hr versus mitochondrial TCA flux of 214 nmol/hr. Dissolved oxygen at 74.2% and a redox ratio of 1.34 frame the metabolic context.
Cell counts across quarters were 16.2, 3.9, 21.2, 19 million, with lactate following at 3, 12.6, 18.5, 6.1 mM. NANOG-high expression was maintained throughout passage 11, and aggregates averaged 364 µm.
Culture format: suspension. Pluripotency marker: NANOG-high. Bioreactor type: spinner-flask. Study: Endo plast.
Endo plast lactate concentration mm: 9.3.
Endo plast: media exchange interval hours — perfusion.

The Sclero cyte experiment utilized a spinner-flask platform for K3-iPSC cells in organoid configuration. Under Inessa Ramanujan's protocol, seeding at 22372 cells/cm² with 3.1 mM glucose yielded 8.8-fold expansion at 96.4% viability over 202 hours.
With 1-13C-glucose labeling, Sclero cyte displayed glycolytic activity of 443 nmol/hr versus mitochondrial TCA flux of 194 nmol/hr. Dissolved oxygen at 60.9% and a redox ratio of 3.96 frame the metabolic context.
Cell counts across quarters were 17.3, 8, 10.8, 8.9 million, with lactate following at 9.2, 12.4, 6, 9.9 mM. OCT4-high expression was maintained throughout passage 50, and aggregates averaged 210 µm.
Culture format: organoid. Pluripotency marker: OCT4-high. Bioreactor type: spinner-flask. Study: Sclero cyte.
Sclero cyte lactate concentration mm: 15.1.

The Sterol kinesis experiment utilized a vertical-wheel platform for KOLF2.1J cells in organoid configuration. Under Orla McBride's protocol, seeding at 47703 cells/cm² with 12.6 mM glucose yielded 11.2-fold expansion at 83.6% viability over 169 hours.
With U-13C5-glutamine labeling, Sterol kinesis displayed glycolytic activity of 256 nmol/hr versus mitochondrial TCA flux of 76 nmol/hr. Dissolved oxygen at 37.1% and a redox ratio of 4.27 frame the metabolic context.
Quarterly expansion for Sterol kinesis: 19.5 → 3.2 → 9.9 → 4.3 million cells. Lactate production tracked at 8.1, 19.6, 5.2, 2.4 mM across the same intervals.
Sterol kinesis — culture format: organoid, pluripotency marker: TRA-1-60, bioreactor type: vertical-wheel.
Sterol kinesis lactate concentration mm: 10.2.
Sterol kinesis aggregate diameter um: 437.
Sterol kinesis: media exchange interval hours — fed-batch.

Redox blast (vertical-wheel, organoid) achieved 9.8-fold expansion from a 53921 cells/cm² seeding density. The PGP1-iPSC line maintained 99.1% viability under Talya Fitzwilliam's optimized protocol with fed-batch-hour media exchange cycles.
Metabolic profiling via U-13C5-glutamine tracing positioned Redox blast at 224 nmol/hr glycolytic flux and 56 nmol/hr TCA cycle throughput. The resulting NAD+/NADH ratio of 1.09 and lactate level of 5.7 mM were recorded at 32.4% oxygen tension.
Cell counts across quarters were 18.6, 22.2, 24.6, 18.3 million, with lactate following at 4.7, 15.2, 14, 14.9 mM. SSEA-4 expression was maintained throughout passage 51, and aggregates averaged 92 µm.
Culture format: organoid. Pluripotency marker: SSEA-4. Bioreactor type: vertical-wheel. Study: Redox blast.
Redox blast glucose concentration mm: 24.2.
Redox blast culture duration hours: 93.

The Priono lemma experiment utilized a hollow-fiber platform for IMR90-iPSC cells in monolayer configuration. Under Kiran Voronova's protocol, seeding at 27413 cells/cm² with 8.7 mM glucose yielded 7.4-fold expansion at 84.1% viability over 236 hours.
With U-13C3-serine labeling, Priono lemma displayed glycolytic activity of 159 nmol/hr versus mitochondrial TCA flux of 387 nmol/hr. Dissolved oxygen at 11.8% and a redox ratio of 1.13 frame the metabolic context.
Cell counts across quarters were 9.8, 6.7, 4.6, 18.9 million, with lactate following at 2.9, 1.5, 14.3, 5.3 mM. KLF4-high expression was maintained throughout passage 32, and aggregates averaged 97 µm.
Priono lemma is characterized by monolayer (culture format), KLF4-high (pluripotency marker), hollow-fiber (bioreactor type).
Priono lemma lactate concentration mm: 0.6.
Priono lemma: media exchange interval hours — fed-batch.

Karyo tome (hollow-fiber, suspension) achieved 10.1-fold expansion from a 40878 cells/cm² seeding density. The IMR90-iPSC line maintained 98% viability under Orla Yamanouchi's optimized protocol with 48-hour media exchange cycles.
Metabolic profiling via U-13C5-glutamine tracing positioned Karyo tome at 348 nmol/hr glycolytic flux and 244 nmol/hr TCA cycle throughput. The resulting NAD+/NADH ratio of 4.27 and lactate level of 12.6 mM were recorded at 18.6% oxygen tension.
Quarterly expansion for Karyo tome: 2.2 → 4.7 → 17.7 → 20.2 million cells. Lactate production tracked at 17.5, 11.6, 3.2, 12.2 mM across the same intervals.
Culture format: suspension. Pluripotency marker: SSEA-4. Bioreactor type: hollow-fiber. Study: Karyo tome.
Karyo tome glucose concentration mm: 3.3.
Karyo tome aggregate diameter um: 242.

Nucleo lysis (fixed-bed, spheroid) achieved 9.6-fold expansion from a 61580 cells/cm² seeding density. The K3-iPSC line maintained 85.5% viability under Leander Lindqvist's optimized protocol with perfusion-hour media exchange cycles.
With U-13C6-glucose labeling, Nucleo lysis displayed glycolytic activity of 209 nmol/hr versus mitochondrial TCA flux of 84 nmol/hr. Dissolved oxygen at 10.6% and a redox ratio of 2.56 frame the metabolic context.
Cell counts across quarters were 17.7, 22.7, 16.7, 3.1 million, with lactate following at 1.1, 16.6, 12.9, 19 mM. NANOG-high expression was maintained throughout passage 56, and aggregates averaged 316 µm.
Nucleo lysis is characterized by spheroid (culture format), NANOG-high (pluripotency marker), fixed-bed (bioreactor type).
Nucleo lysis glucose concentration mm: 14.5.
Nucleo lysis lactate concentration mm: 4.9.
Nucleo lysis culture duration hours: 49.

Neuro plast (fixed-bed, spheroid) achieved 4.9-fold expansion from a 10252 cells/cm² seeding density. The BJ-iPSC line maintained 78.3% viability under Riva Petridis's optimized protocol with 72-hour media exchange cycles.
With U-13C3-pyruvate labeling, Neuro plast displayed glycolytic activity of 195 nmol/hr versus mitochondrial TCA flux of 333 nmol/hr. Dissolved oxygen at 74.1% and a redox ratio of 4.54 frame the metabolic context.
Cell counts across quarters were 5.6, 2.5, 16.6, 24.4 million, with lactate following at 8.6, 6.4, 15.4, 16.7 mM. TRA-1-60 expression was maintained throughout passage 54, and aggregates averaged 353 µm.
Culture format: spheroid. Pluripotency marker: TRA-1-60. Bioreactor type: fixed-bed. Study: Neuro plast.
Neuro plast glucose concentration mm: 2.2.
Neuro plast culture duration hours: 106.

The Osmo pulse experiment utilized a hollow-fiber platform for GM25256 cells in encapsulated configuration. Under Vesna Okamura's protocol, seeding at 8618 cells/cm² with 24.6 mM glucose yielded 2.9-fold expansion at 95.8% viability over 99 hours.
Metabolic profiling via 1-13C-glucose tracing positioned Osmo pulse at 142 nmol/hr glycolytic flux and 217 nmol/hr TCA cycle throughput. The resulting NAD+/NADH ratio of 4.77 and lactate level of 2.8 mM were recorded at 45.7% oxygen tension.
Cell counts across quarters were 17.3, 1.2, 9.3, 16 million, with lactate following at 15.3, 15.5, 11.6, 8.7 mM. SOX2-high expression was maintained throughout passage 11, and aggregates averaged 141 µm.
Culture format: encapsulated. Pluripotency marker: SOX2-high. Bioreactor type: hollow-fiber. Study: Osmo pulse.
Osmo pulse: media exchange interval hours — continuous.

The Axono blast experiment utilized a spinner-flask platform for 201B7 cells in microcarrier configuration. Under Hessa Yamanouchi's protocol, seeding at 18679 cells/cm² with 5.2 mM glucose yielded 9.6-fold expansion at 78.6% viability over 152 hours.
Metabolic profiling via 2-13C-acetate tracing positioned Axono blast at 589 nmol/hr glycolytic flux and 94 nmol/hr TCA cycle throughput. The resulting NAD+/NADH ratio of 6.11 and lactate level of 7.4 mM were recorded at 80.7% oxygen tension.
Quarterly expansion for Axono blast: 19.6 → 6.7 → 19.8 → 12.7 million cells. Lactate production tracked at 19.1, 10.9, 9.6, 15.3 mM across the same intervals.
Axono blast — culture format: microcarrier, pluripotency marker: SSEA-4, bioreactor type: spinner-flask.
Axono blast aggregate diameter um: 133.
Axono blast passage number: 44.
Axono blast: media exchange interval hours — 72.
The TCA cycle throughput of Chrono blast surpasses that of Osmo pulse by 26743 nmol/hr.

Meso lysis (rocking-motion, microcarrier) achieved 7.8-fold expansion from a 67787 cells/cm² seeding density. The 1383D6 line maintained 88% viability under Niall Osei-Mensah's optimized protocol with 72-hour media exchange cycles.
The TCA cycle throughput of Redox blast surpasses that of Chrono blast by 6 nmol/hr.
Steady-state flux analysis, validated by enrichment plateau criteria, fixed Priono lemma's glycolytic rate at 159 nmol/hr.
With U-13C5-glutamine labeling, Meso lysis displayed glycolytic activity of 486 nmol/hr versus mitochondrial TCA flux of 101 nmol/hr. Dissolved oxygen at 23.2% and a redox ratio of 5.31 frame the metabolic context.
Upon reaching isotopic enrichment plateau at steady state, Priono lemma's confirmed glycolytic flux was established at 159 nmol/hr.
Quarterly expansion for Meso lysis: 8.1 → 21 → 10.7 → 3.5 million cells. Lactate production tracked at 15.1, 14.6, 13, 18.2 mM across the same intervals.
Meso lysis is characterized by microcarrier (culture format), OCT4-high (pluripotency marker), rocking-motion (bioreactor type).
Meso lysis glucose concentration mm: 6.7.
Meso lysis lactate concentration mm: 12.4.
Meso lysis aggregate diameter um: 413.
Meso lysis culture duration hours: 102.

Ribo pulse (stirred-tank, monolayer) achieved 9.2-fold expansion from a 50122 cells/cm² seeding density. The GM25256 line maintained 84% viability under Hessa Szabo's optimized protocol with 72-hour media exchange cycles.
Metabolic profiling via 3-13C-lactate tracing positioned Ribo pulse at 468 nmol/hr glycolytic flux and 316 nmol/hr TCA cycle throughput. The resulting NAD+/NADH ratio of 4.74 and lactate level of 13.1 mM were recorded at 17.7% oxygen tension.
Quarterly expansion for Ribo pulse: 16.6 → 23 → 13 → 10.2 million cells. Lactate production tracked at 9.2, 12, 6.6, 2.7 mM across the same intervals.
Ribo pulse — culture format: monolayer, pluripotency marker: SSEA-4, bioreactor type: stirred-tank.
Ribo pulse aggregate diameter um: 446.
Ribo pulse passage number: 48.
Ribo pulse culture duration hours: 88.

trapLocation.injectedText: Lipido plasm (fixed-bed, monolayer) achieved 11.6-fold expansion from a 68596 cells/cm² seeding density. The IMR90-iPSC line maintained 97.7% viability under Katia Szabo's optimized protocol with 12-h

questions.text: Among all studies, which showed the most compromised cell viability?
Summing quarterly cell counts, which study produced the highest cumulative cell yield?
Averaging across all four quarters, which study maintained the lowest mean lactate output?
Across all studies, which exhibited the greatest absolute shift in lactate production from Q1 to Q4?
Among studies with the KLF4-high pluripotency marker, which had the lowest TCA cycle flux?
Of all organoid-format studies, which was seeded at the lowest initial cell density?
Which study grew the most cells on average across quarters? Identify its cell line, then among studies with the same cell line, find the one with the strongest TCA flux. What passage is it at?
Which study exhibits the most suppressed glycolysis? Determine its cell line, then among studies using that line find the best fold expansion. Report its seeding density.
Among studies using the suspension format, which had the highest glycolytic flux?
Which study was initiated at the smallest seeding density (cells/cm²)?

document.text:
Process Transfer Readiness: iPSC Culture Scale-Up Performance Evaluation

Tropho trophy: GM25256 in microcarrier format, vertical-wheel bioreactor at 34610 cells/cm². Media exchange every fed-batch hours with 10.7 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 11.3%. Total run time: 64 hours. PI: Oskar Ferrante.
Tropho trophy achieved 16.9 million cells by Q4 with 87.9% viability and 7.5-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (U-13C5-glutamine tracing): glycolytic flux 492 nmol/hr, TCA flux 291 nmol/hr, redox ratio 3.05, lactate 16.1 mM.
Tropho trophy is characterized by microcarrier (culture format), TRA-1-60 (pluripotency marker), vertical-wheel (bioreactor type).
Tropho trophy aggregate diameter um: 353.
Tropho trophy quarterly cell count million: Q1=11.1, Q2=17.3, Q3=8.9, Q4=16.9.
Tropho trophy quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=3.2, Q2=13.8, Q3=12.2, Q4=18.8.

Process sheet for Mito cyte (Jorin McBride): KOLF2.1J cells seeded at 22228/cm² in microcarrier configuration within a stirred-tank vessel. Glucose at 24.5 mM, O₂ at 69.6%, fed-batch-hour feed cycle, 81-hour duration.
Expansion yielded 8.3-fold increase at 83.6% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 5.8, 2.7, 15.9, 1.9 million. Aggregate diameter: 333 µm. SSEA-4 confirmed at passage 29. Lactate quarterly: 4.6, 12.8, 12.8, 7.1 mM.
Culture format: microcarrier. Pluripotency marker: SSEA-4. Bioreactor type: stirred-tank. Study: Mito cyte.
Mito cyte lactate concentration mm: 11.2.
Mito cyte glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 571.
Mito cyte tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 325.
Mito cyte nad nadh redox ratio: 5.45.
Mito cyte: isotope tracer — 3-13C-lactate.

Phago kinesis: 1383D6 in spheroid format, vertical-wheel bioreactor at 55935 cells/cm². Media exchange every 72 hours with 23 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 51.1%. Total run time: 82 hours. PI: Inessa Mukherjee.
Expansion yielded 4.9-fold increase at 94.8% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 16.4, 23.9, 6.5, 17.8 million. Aggregate diameter: 115 µm. NANOG-high confirmed at passage 14. Lactate quarterly: 18.5, 3.5, 11.6, 4.9 mM.
Culture format: spheroid. Pluripotency marker: NANOG-high. Bioreactor type: vertical-wheel. Study: Phago kinesis.
Phago kinesis lactate concentration mm: 0.9.
Preliminary flux estimates from early-timepoint sampling placed Proto tome's glycolytic rate at 273 nmol/hr, prior to isotopic enrichment reaching steady state.
Phago kinesis glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 683.
Phago kinesis tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 367.
Phago kinesis nad nadh redox ratio: 6.29.
Phago kinesis: isotope tracer — U-13C6-glucose.

Process sheet for Cyto soma (Darya Chaturvedi): 201B7 cells seeded at 21063/cm² in monolayer configuration within a fixed-bed vessel. Glucose at 3.7 mM, O₂ at 48.4%, 72-hour feed cycle, 64-hour duration.
Cyto soma achieved 11.3 million cells by Q4 with 80.5% viability and 2.9-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (U-13C6-glucose tracing): glycolytic flux 773 nmol/hr, TCA flux 256 nmol/hr, redox ratio 1.55, lactate 12.7 mM.
Culture format: monolayer. Pluripotency marker: KLF4-high. Bioreactor type: fixed-bed. Study: Cyto soma.
Cyto soma aggregate diameter um: 398.
Cyto soma quarterly cell count million: Q1=17.1, Q2=13, Q3=24.6, Q4=11.3.
Cyto soma quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=14.5, Q2=3.9, Q3=19.7, Q4=9.9.

Neuro graft: GM25256 in scaffold format, hollow-fiber bioreactor at 64990 cells/cm². Media exchange every perfusion hours with 18.3 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 37.7%. Total run time: 166 hours. PI: Riva Delvaux.
Expansion yielded 10.2-fold increase at 87.3% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 15.3, 9.8, 11.3, 3.9 million. Aggregate diameter: 169 µm. NANOG-high confirmed at passage 17. Lactate quarterly: 13.1, 7.2, 5.7, 13.4 mM.
Neuro graft — culture format: scaffold, pluripotency marker: NANOG-high, bioreactor type: hollow-fiber.
Neuro graft lactate concentration mm: 14.9.
Neuro graft glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 742.
Neuro graft tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 108.
Neuro graft nad nadh redox ratio: 0.93.
Neuro graft: isotope tracer — U-13C3-pyruvate.

Process sheet for Ribo merge (Petra Dimitriou): WTC-11 cells seeded at 31763/cm² in microcarrier configuration within a spinner-flask vessel. Glucose at 18.1 mM, O₂ at 74.4%, 36-hour feed cycle, 214-hour duration.
Expansion yielded 4.3-fold increase at 84.7% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 10.4, 20.4, 20.5, 8.9 million. Aggregate diameter: 254 µm. SOX2-high confirmed at passage 39. Lactate quarterly: 3.4, 19.4, 17.8, 8.2 mM.
Ribo merge — culture format: microcarrier, pluripotency marker: SOX2-high, bioreactor type: spinner-flask.
Ribo merge lactate concentration mm: 11.4.
Ribo merge glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 707.
Ribo merge tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 102.
Ribo merge nad nadh redox ratio: 7.25.
Ribo merge: isotope tracer — 1-13C-glucose.

Nucleo tome: 201B7 in spheroid format, orbital-shaker bioreactor at 54347 cells/cm². Media exchange every 24 hours with 22.1 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 63.4%. Total run time: 216 hours. PI: Niall Delvaux.
Expansion yielded 6.8-fold increase at 96% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 5.6, 13.7, 17.3, 15.4 million. Aggregate diameter: 138 µm. DPPA4-high confirmed at passage 34. Lactate quarterly: 14.3, 18.1, 5.9, 14.6 mM.
Nucleo tome is characterized by spheroid (culture format), DPPA4-high (pluripotency marker), orbital-shaker (bioreactor type).
Nucleo tome lactate concentration mm: 9.1.
Nucleo tome glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 880.
Nucleo tome tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 304.
Nucleo tome nad nadh redox ratio: 7.97.
Nucleo tome: isotope tracer — U-13C3-serine.

Dendro stasis: PGP1-iPSC in scaffold format, microfluidic bioreactor at 55778 cells/cm². Media exchange every continuous hours with 14.2 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 66.1%. Total run time: 91 hours. PI: Ziven Fitzwilliam.
Dendro stasis achieved 17.3 million cells by Q4 with 82% viability and 8.1-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (2-13C-acetate tracing): glycolytic flux 810 nmol/hr, TCA flux 223 nmol/hr, redox ratio 1.84, lactate 4.1 mM.
Dendro stasis is characterized by scaffold (culture format), NANOG-high (pluripotency marker), microfluidic (bioreactor type).
Dendro stasis aggregate diameter um: 421.
Dendro stasis passage number: 50.
Dendro stasis quarterly cell count million: Q1=0.7, Q2=11.3, Q3=21.5, Q4=17.3.
Dendro stasis quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=15, Q2=14.6, Q3=4.4, Q4=16.3.

Process sheet for Proto tome (Nessa Osei-Mensah): IMR90-iPSC cells seeded at 10536/cm² in microcarrier configuration within a rocking-motion vessel. Glucose at 8.5 mM, O₂ at 25.8%, 48-hour feed cycle, 208-hour duration.
Expansion yielded 2.6-fold increase at 94.9% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 8, 1.7, 14.5, 11.1 million. Aggregate diameter: 134 µm. OCT4-high confirmed at passage 62. Lactate quarterly: 12.2, 12.6, 6.1, 6.6 mM.
Proto tome is characterized by microcarrier (culture format), OCT4-high (pluripotency marker), rocking-motion (bioreactor type).
Proto tome lactate concentration mm: 17.4.
Proto tome glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 214.
Proto tome tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 85.
Proto tome nad nadh redox ratio: 1.13.
Proto tome: isotope tracer — U-13C3-pyruvate.

Process sheet for Lami merge (Oskar Voronova): GM25256 cells seeded at 30512/cm² in encapsulated configuration within a vertical-wheel vessel. Glucose at 16.9 mM, O₂ at 12%, 72-hour feed cycle, 68-hour duration.
Lami merge achieved 18.8 million cells by Q4 with 95.4% viability and 3.8-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (U-13C6-glucose tracing): glycolytic flux 842 nmol/hr, TCA flux 172 nmol/hr, redox ratio 1.12, lactate 4.2 mM.
Culture format: encapsulated. Pluripotency marker: DPPA4-high. Bioreactor type: vertical-wheel. Study: Lami merge.
Lami merge aggregate diameter um: 99.
Lami merge passage number: 38.
Synap lysis was seeded at 10 cells/cm² above Ribo merge.
Lami merge quarterly cell count million: Q1=10.3, Q2=2.5, Q3=10.5, Q4=18.8.
Lami merge quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=4.9, Q2=16.5, Q3=5.6, Q4=6.4.

Redox scope: PGP1-iPSC in organoid format, fixed-bed bioreactor at 48546 cells/cm². Media exchange every 12 hours with 4.7 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 58.8%. Total run time: 61 hours. PI: Cosima Steinfeld.
Redox scope achieved 12.1 million cells by Q4 with 87.2% viability and 7.8-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (1-13C-glucose tracing): glycolytic flux 853 nmol/hr, TCA flux 160 nmol/hr, redox ratio 1.78, lactate 17 mM.
Redox scope — culture format: organoid, pluripotency marker: OCT4-high, bioreactor type: fixed-bed.
Redox scope aggregate diameter um: 144.
Redox scope passage number: 11.
Redox scope quarterly cell count million: Q1=0.9, Q2=8.2, Q3=24.7, Q4=12.1.
Redox scope quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=16.2, Q2=6.3, Q3=2.1, Q4=2.4.

Process sheet for Kineto clast (Dagny Dimitriou): 201B7 cells seeded at 48034/cm² in suspension configuration within a orbital-shaker vessel. Glucose at 8.4 mM, O₂ at 68.4%, 72-hour feed cycle, 96-hour duration.
Expansion yielded 9.5-fold increase at 87.5% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 7, 3.5, 19.4, 1.6 million. Aggregate diameter: 101 µm. KLF4-high confirmed at passage 28. Lactate quarterly: 16, 17.4, 10.3, 9.5 mM.
Kineto clast is characterized by suspension (culture format), KLF4-high (pluripotency marker), orbital-shaker (bioreactor type).
Kineto clast lactate concentration mm: 2.7.
Kineto clast glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 475.
Kineto clast tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 277.
Kineto clast nad nadh redox ratio: 3.79.
Kineto clast: isotope tracer — 3-13C-lactate.

Steady-state flux analysis, validated by enrichment plateau criteria, fixed Proto tome's glycolytic rate at 214 nmol/hr.
Process sheet for Synap lysis (Eshan Fitzwilliam): IMR90-iPSC cells seeded at 45954/cm² in scaffold configuration within a hollow-fiber vessel. Glucose at 2.8 mM, O₂ at 47.3%, perfusion-hour feed cycle, 212-hour duration.
Synap lysis achieved 9.2 million cells by Q4 with 78.5% viability and 8.4-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (3-13C-lactate tracing): glycolytic flux 446 nmol/hr, TCA flux 92 nmol/hr, redox ratio 1.77, lactate 16.2 mM.
Synap lysis is characterized by scaffold (culture format), SOX2-high (pluripotency marker), hollow-fiber (bioreactor type).
Synap lysis aggregate diameter um: 84.
Synap lysis passage number: 37.
Synap lysis quarterly cell count million: Q1=6.2, Q2=15, Q3=18.4, Q4=9.2.
Tropho trophy's glycolytic flux exceeds Ribo merge's by 215 nmol/hr.
Upon reaching isotopic enrichment plateau at steady state, Proto tome's confirmed glycolytic flux was established at 214 nmol/hr.
Synap lysis quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=3.1, Q2=7.3, Q3=5, Q4=7.

Cilio kinesis: KOLF2.1J in organoid format, fixed-bed bioreactor at 34321 cells/cm². Media exchange every 36 hours with 4.7 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 49.7%. Total run time: 64 hours. PI: Ulric Kowalczyk.
Cilio kinesis achieved 3.2 million cells by Q4 with 79.9% viability and 11.5-fold 72-hour expansion. Metabolic indicators (1-13C-glucose tracing): glycolytic flux 346 nmol/hr, TCA flux 244 nmol/hr, redox ratio 1.28, lactate 0.2 mM.
Cilio kinesis — culture format: organoid, pluripotency marker: REX1-high, bioreactor type: fixed-bed.
Cilio kinesis aggregate diameter um: 307.
Cilio kinesis passage number: 63.
Cilio kinesis quarterly cell count million: Q1=18.4, Q2=0.6, Q3=9.6, Q4=3.2.
Cilio kinesis quarterly lactate production mm: Q1=18, Q2=13.7, Q3=6.1, Q4=17.3.

Baro tome: WTC-11 in microcarrier format, stirred-tank bioreactor at 38555 cells/cm². Media exchange every 24 hours with 24.9 mM glucose. Target dissolved oxygen: 80.6%. Total run time: 190 hours. PI: Emrik Okonkwo.
Expansion yielded 9.9-fold increase at 86.2% viability. Quarterly cell counts: 1.2, 14.2, 6.2, 12.8 million. Aggregate diameter: 435 µm. NANOG-high confirmed at passage 26. Lactate quarterly: 18.8, 15.9, 3.7, 6.5 mM.
Culture format: microcarrier. Pluripotency marker: NANOG-high. Bioreactor type: stirred-tank. Study: Baro tome.
Baro tome lactate concentration mm: 14.6.
Baro tome glycolytic flux nmol per hr: 678.
Baro tome tca cycle flux nmol per hr: 157.
Baro tome nad nadh redox ratio: 3.18.
Baro tome: isotope tracer — 1-13C-glucose.
