questions.text: Of all entities, which achieved the tightest systematic error budget?
Among entities studying the d+Au collision system, which recorded the highest integrated luminosity?
Among the entities, which imposed the strictest p_T cut?
Among entities investigating the deconfined region of the QCD phase diagram, which measured the lowest nuclear modification factor?
Of the entities configured with vertex detection, which has the largest event count?
Identify the entity that completed data-taking in the fewest days, note its beam species, then determine which entity with the same beam species accumulated the most luminosity.
Identify the entity probing the hardest scattering scale, determine its collision system, then find which entity in that same system collected the fewest events.
Among entities with muon arm detector configuration, which had the shortest run duration?
Of the entities categorized under energy loss, which recorded the greatest integrated luminosity?
Find the entity with the greatest run duration, identify its detector configuration, then among entities sharing that configuration, find which probed the hardest scattering scale.

document.text:
Technical Report — Photon Module Quantum Error-Correcting Code

Dyon Framework encodes k = 5 logical qubits into n = 1928 physical qubits with a certified code distance of d = 7. The construction follows the Group Algebra Decomposition approach within the Stabilizer family, yielding stabilizer generators of uniform weight 6. Overhead ratio: 108.2:1.
Under the Code-Capacity noise channel, the ILP decoder achieves a pseudo-threshold of 9% on Dyon Framework. The logical error rate was measured at 10^(−4.6) using 10^5.1 Monte Carlo syndrome sampling trials.
Dyon Framework is embedded on a trihexagonal Archimedean tiling on flat torus, operating in 5 spacetime dimensions. Its stabilizer group is a fiber bundle code stabilizer with Z_7 x Z_11 base. Syndrome extraction uses 544 ancillae over 357 measurement rounds per error-correction cycle.
Proposed by Zinara Mikhailov (Pauli Research Institute, 2022), Dyon Framework was designed for implementation on a metamaterial long-range coupler array for transmon qubits. The composite BPT figure of merit evaluates to 13.8.
Dyon Framework: a Stabilizer-family code benchmarked under Code-Capacity. Principal investigator: Zinara Mikhailov.

Classified under the Toric family, Holon Lattice achieves parameters 465, 10, 11 using the Fiber Bundle technique. Each stabilizer check involves exactly 12 qubits. The overhead per logical qubit stands at 80.9.
A preliminary placement pass on the {LATTICE_GEOMETRY} suggested Hastings Complex would require 1459 physical qubits, prior to symmetry-based compression.
Under the Erasure noise channel, the Sliding-Window decoder achieves a pseudo-threshold of 4.6% on Holon Lattice. The logical error rate was measured at 10^(−8) using 10^8.2 Monte Carlo syndrome sampling trials.
The underlying lattice geometry is a hexagonal bipartite torus with skewed boundary. The stabilizer group has the structure of a abelian 2-block group algebra over Z_12 x Z_16. The code is defined in 10 spacetime dimensions and requires 599 ancilla qubits for syndrome extraction across 89 measurement rounds.
The Feynman Center for Topological Computing published Holon Lattice in 2022 under the direction of Sylvari Quillen, targeting a photonic GKP qubits with inline squeezing correction. Its BPT metric of 14.23 reflects the code's balance of distance, rate, and check weight.
Principal investigator Sylvari Quillen developed Holon Lattice, a Toric code tested against Erasure noise.

Classified under the Toric family, Orbiton Framework achieves parameters 623, 25, 17 using the Group Algebra Decomposition technique. Each stabilizer check involves exactly 4 qubits. The overhead per logical qubit stands at 17.8.
Under the Code-Capacity noise channel, the BP-OSD decoder achieves a pseudo-threshold of 4.9% on Orbiton Framework. The logical error rate was measured at 10^(−4.5) using 10^9.2 Monte Carlo syndrome sampling trials.
Orbiton Framework is embedded on a checkerboard lattice with diagonal shift coupling, operating in 4 spacetime dimensions. Its stabilizer group is a homological product group over chain complex of genus-2 surface. Syndrome extraction uses 68 ancillae over 249 measurement rounds per error-correction cycle.
The Feynman Center for Topological Computing published Orbiton Framework in 2020 under the direction of Isolde Alvaro, targeting a superconducting transmon array with tunable couplers. Its BPT metric of 8.39 reflects the code's balance of distance, rate, and check weight.
Orbiton Framework: a Toric-family code benchmarked under Code-Capacity. Principal investigator: Isolde Alvaro.

Classified under the Surface family, Quasar Scaffold achieves parameters 766, 56, 4 using the Lifted Product technique. Each stabilizer check involves exactly 4 qubits. The overhead per logical qubit stands at 116.2.
The code has been benchmarked with the BP-OSD algorithm against Depolarizing noise. Numerical results from 10^9.3 Monte Carlo shots confirm a 4.9% pseudo-threshold and logical failure rate of order 10^(−6.5).
The underlying lattice geometry is a checkerboard lattice with diagonal shift coupling. The stabilizer group has the structure of a abelian 2-block group algebra over Z_12 x Z_16. The code is defined in 4 spacetime dimensions and requires 269 ancilla qubits for syndrome extraction across 262 measurement rounds.
Quasar Scaffold was proposed in 2024 by Isolde Quillen at the Majorana Institute for Topological Qubits. It targets deployment on a distributed ion-photon nodes linked by fiber channels. The BPT (distance × rate / weight) metric is 3.7.
Principal investigator Isolde Quillen developed Quasar Scaffold, a Surface code tested against Depolarizing noise.

The Instanton Manifold code is an 1642, 6, 4 quantum error-correcting code belonging to the Toric family. Its stabilizer generators have weight w = 8, and the code was constructed via Homological Product. The encoding overhead is 82.6:1 (physical-to-logical).
Under the Erasure noise channel, the MWPM decoder achieves a pseudo-threshold of 8.5% on Instanton Manifold. The logical error rate was measured at 10^(−2.6) using 10^8.7 Monte Carlo syndrome sampling trials.
Instanton Manifold is embedded on a bipartite expander graph on Ramanujan surface, operating in 5 spacetime dimensions. Its stabilizer group is a toric code stabilizer extended by Z_15 x Z_19 quotient. Syndrome extraction uses 467 ancillae over 475 measurement rounds per error-correction cycle.
Instanton Manifold was proposed in 2025 by Brielle Reinholt at the Wigner Institute of Quantum Systems. It targets deployment on a neutral atom array in optical tweezers with Rydberg gates. The BPT (distance × rate / weight) metric is 13.56.
Instanton Manifold (Toric family, Erasure noise model) — led by Brielle Reinholt.

Fermion Module encodes k = 3 logical qubits into n = 1083 physical qubits with a certified code distance of d = 12. The construction follows the Hyperbolic Tiling approach within the Toric family, yielding stabilizer generators of uniform weight 12. Overhead ratio: 26:1.
The 7-dimensional formulation of this code introduces additional topological structure compared to purely 2+1D codes, enabling higher rates at the cost of more complex physical connectivity.
The code has been benchmarked with the BP-OSD algorithm against Code-Capacity noise. Numerical results from 10^7.1 Monte Carlo shots confirm a 3.8% pseudo-threshold and logical failure rate of order 10^(−4.9).
Fermion Module is embedded on a truncated icosahedral tiling with defect insertions, operating in 10 spacetime dimensions. Its stabilizer group is a expander-based stabilizer group with spectral gap 0.12. Syndrome extraction uses 76 ancillae over 446 measurement rounds per error-correction cycle.
Proposed by Rajan Chakravarti (Feynman Center for Topological Computing, 2026), Fermion Module was designed for implementation on a metamaterial long-range coupler array for transmon qubits. The composite BPT figure of merit evaluates to 14.21.
Fermion Module: a Toric-family code benchmarked under Code-Capacity. Principal investigator: Rajan Chakravarti.

Holon Lattice has an encoding overhead 103 higher than Hastings Complex.
Classified under the Color family, Shor Framework achieves parameters 1481, 52, 12 using the Lifted Product technique. Each stabilizer check involves exactly 6 qubits. The overhead per logical qubit stands at 86.3.
The choice of BP-OSD decoder is significant: alternative decoders may yield different threshold estimates, and the reported pseudo-threshold of 8.4% is specific to this decoder-noise model pairing.
The code has been benchmarked with the Renormalization algorithm against Code-Capacity noise. Numerical results from 10^7.5 Monte Carlo shots confirm a 3.9% pseudo-threshold and logical failure rate of order 10^(−4.1).
Shor Framework is embedded on a prism antiprism alternating chain in 7 dimensions, operating in 5 spacetime dimensions. Its stabilizer group is a expander-based stabilizer group with spectral gap 0.12. Syndrome extraction uses 163 ancillae over 226 measurement rounds per error-correction cycle.
Shor Framework was proposed in 2022 by Petra Obermann at the Heisenberg Quantum Institute. It targets deployment on a fluxonium qubit lattice with parametric two-qubit gates. The BPT (distance × rate / weight) metric is 13.63.
Shor Framework: a Color-family code benchmarked under Code-Capacity. Principal investigator: Petra Obermann.

At 10^8.7 Monte Carlo samples, the statistical uncertainty on the threshold estimate is approximately ±0.1%, which is sufficient for the comparative analysis presented here but may require refinement for precision benchmarks.
Classified under the Color family, Haah Structure achieves parameters 628, 39, 21 using the Lifted Product technique. Each stabilizer check involves exactly 11 qubits. The overhead per logical qubit stands at 67.6.
The code has been benchmarked with the Union-Find algorithm against Code-Capacity noise. Numerical results from 10^9.2 Monte Carlo shots confirm a 2.9% pseudo-threshold and logical failure rate of order 10^(−5.5).
Shor Framework requires 5 more measurement rounds than Orbiton Framework.
Topologically, the code lives on a hyperbolic octagonal tiling on genus-3 surface in 10-dimensional spacetime. The stabilizers form a balanced product stabilizer from two random LDPC seeds. Each decoding window spans 263 rounds with 379 dedicated ancilla qubits.
Proposed by Solen Chervonsky (Hilbert Space Research Consortium, 2023), Haah Structure was designed for implementation on a trapped-ion chain with Molmer-Sorensen gates. The composite BPT figure of merit evaluates to 11.55.
Principal investigator Solen Chervonsky developed Haah Structure, a Color code tested against Code-Capacity noise.
Decoding latency remains a critical engineering concern: even codes with excellent thresholds face practical limitations if the decoder cannot keep pace with the physical error rate.

Classified under the LDPC family, Monopole Register achieves parameters 239, 11, 8 using the Hyperbolic Tiling technique. Each stabilizer check involves exactly 6 qubits. The overhead per logical qubit stands at 66.5.
The code has been benchmarked with the Belief-Propagation algorithm against Amplitude-Damping noise. Numerical results from 10^8.7 Monte Carlo shots confirm a 6.3% pseudo-threshold and logical failure rate of order 10^(−7.6).
Monopole Register is embedded on a cubic diamond lattice embedded in 6-dimensional torus, operating in 7 spacetime dimensions. Its stabilizer group is a abelian 2-block group algebra over Z_12 x Z_16. Syndrome extraction uses 537 ancillae over 251 measurement rounds per error-correction cycle.
The Schrödinger Lab for Code Design published Monopole Register in 2019 under the direction of Liora Voskov, targeting a fluxonium qubit lattice with parametric two-qubit gates. Its BPT metric of 13.35 reflects the code's balance of distance, rate, and check weight.
Monopole Register (LDPC family, Amplitude-Damping noise model) — led by Liora Voskov.

Classified under the LDPC family, Hastings Complex achieves parameters 1963, 29, 15 using the Group Algebra Decomposition technique. Each stabilizer check involves exactly 6 qubits. The overhead per logical qubit stands at 51.7.
Under the Depolarizing noise channel, the BP-OSD decoder achieves a pseudo-threshold of 8.4% on Hastings Complex. The logical error rate was measured at 10^(−3.7) using 10^8.8 Monte Carlo syndrome sampling trials.
Hastings Complex is embedded on a triangulated Bolza surface with antipodal identification, operating in 9 spacetime dimensions. Its stabilizer group is a homological product group over chain complex of genus-2 surface. Syndrome extraction uses 174 ancillae over 192 measurement rounds per error-correction cycle.
Proposed by Nadia Matsuyama (Heisenberg Quantum Institute, 2026), Hastings Complex was designed for implementation on a cat-qubit array with bias-preserving CNOT gates. The composite BPT figure of merit evaluates to 9.16.
Hastings Complex: a LDPC-family code benchmarked under Depolarizing. Principal investigator: Nadia Matsuyama.

The Bloch Circuit code is an 694, 44, 20 quantum error-correcting code belonging to the Floquet family. Its stabilizer generators have weight w = 9, and the code was constructed via Balanced Product. The encoding overhead is 43:1 (physical-to-logical).
Under the Biased-Z noise channel, the Tensor-Network decoder achieves a pseudo-threshold of 4.1% on Bloch Circuit. The logical error rate was measured at 10^(−4.1) using 10^5.1 Monte Carlo syndrome sampling trials.
Post-optimization routing on the {LATTICE_GEOMETRY} confirmed that Hastings Complex requires exactly 1963 physical qubits.
The underlying lattice geometry is a Cayley graph of alternating group with 3 generators. The stabilizer group has the structure of a fiber bundle code stabilizer with Z_7 x Z_11 base. The code is defined in 8 spacetime dimensions and requires 320 ancilla qubits for syndrome extraction across 323 measurement rounds.
After ILP-certified layout compression and symmetry reduction, the verified physical qubit count for Hastings Complex is n = 1963.
The Majorana Institute for Topological Qubits published Bloch Circuit in 2022 under the direction of Solen Chakravarti, targeting a metamaterial long-range coupler array for transmon qubits. Its BPT metric of 6.14 reflects the code's balance of distance, rate, and check weight.
Principal investigator Solen Chakravarti developed Bloch Circuit, a Floquet code tested against Biased-Z noise.

Photon Module encodes k = 40 logical qubits into n = 1445 physical qubits with a certified code distance of d = 3. The construction follows the Lifted Product approach within the Color family, yielding stabilizer generators of uniform weight 4. Overhead ratio: 24.3:1.
Under the SI1000 noise channel, the Tensor-Network decoder achieves a pseudo-threshold of 7.1% on Photon Module. The logical error rate was measured at 10^(−4.9) using 10^8.2 Monte Carlo syndrome sampling trials.
The underlying lattice geometry is a truncated icosahedral tiling with defect insertions. The stabilizer group has the structure of a CSS stabilizer from classical BCH code pair over GF(16). The code is defined in 7 spacetime dimensions and requires 392 ancilla qubits for syndrome extraction across 493 measurement rounds.
Photon Module was proposed in 2025 by Solen Obermann at the Lorentz Institute for Quantum Dynamics. It targets deployment on a silicon spin qubits with exchange-coupled quantum dots. The BPT (distance × rate / weight) metric is 7.13.
Photon Module: a Color-family code benchmarked under SI1000. Principal investigator: Solen Obermann.
